seoTitle: How to Add, Remove, or Change a Dependent in TurboTax
metaDescription: Learn how to add, remove, or edit dependent information in TurboTax so your deductions and credits reflect your current household accurately.
intro: Your dependents directly affect your deductions and credits, so keeping that information current in TurboTax matters at tax time. This guide walks through adding a new dependent, removing one, and editing existing information. It's useful for freelancers, small business owners, and anyone whose household situation has changed since their last filing.

steps.heading: Add a dependent
steps.body: Enter the required information to add a new dependent to your TurboTax return.

steps.heading: Remove a dependent
steps.body: Follow the steps to remove a dependent who no longer qualifies or should no longer be listed on your return.

steps.heading: Edit existing dependent information
steps.body: Update any details for a current dependent that have changed since your last filing.

steps.heading: Review the tax benefits tied to dependents
steps.body: Understand how adding or changing dependents affects your deductions and credits before finalizing your return.

faq.question: How do I know if someone qualifies as my dependent?
faq.answer: IRS rules determine dependent eligibility based on factors like relationship, residency, and support; TurboTax will guide you through the qualifying questions as you enter dependent information.

faq.question: Can I remove a dependent after already filing?
faq.answer: If you've already filed, you'd typically need to file an amended return rather than editing the original submission directly.

faq.question: Does changing a dependent affect my refund?
faq.answer: Yes, adding or removing a dependent can change your eligibility for certain deductions and credits, which may affect your refund or amount owed.

faq.question: Is this process the same for freelancers and traditional employees?
faq.answer: Yes, the dependent entry process in TurboTax is the same regardless of your income type, since it's based on your household situation rather than employment status.
